comment.narrative: Zero

• 10 Expert bill reviews • 5 Public Consultations • 17 Parliamentary oversight • 50 Orientation/interactions/meeting/workshop • 4 Meet the speakers • 20 episodes of radio programme • Functional of SDGs-resource center

11 Expert bill reviews including post legislative scrutiny of three act( discriminations, anticorruption and environment) 7 Public consultation were conducted on bills Supported parliamentary committees to conduct 17 Parliamentary oversight missions 60 Orientation/interactions/meeting/workshop conducted to enhance capacity of MPs and parliament secretariat staff 4 meet the speakers programme conducted 24 Episode of radio programme conducted on parliamentary issues Technical support provided to SDG resource center for smooth functioning. 8 Policy dialogue conducted by the Sustainable Development and Good Governance committee on SDG agendas .

4 Expert bill reviews • 7 Public Consultations 2 Post Legislative assessment/Scrutiny • 18 Parliamentary oversight • 25 Orientation/interactions/meeting/workshop • 10 Meet the speakers • 20 episodes of radio programme 1 ICT Support ( VCF installation) • 4 SDG Policy Dialogue and 4 SDG implementation monitoring

4 Expert bill reviews conducted • 7 Public Consultations completed • 2 Post Legislative assessment/Scrutiny + 1 assessments of 10 acts of PAs + development of a model bill completed • 18 Parliamentary oversight completed • 56 Orientation/interactions/meeting/workshop conducted • 12 events of Meet the speakers completed • 25 episodes of radio programme conducted • 5 SDG Policy Dialogue and 4 SDG implementation monitoring

Interaction between OAG and PACs=7 interactions Support FP Committees for expert review and public consultations on 2 bills Support PA Committees for expert review and public consultations on 5 bills Support to PA Committees for Parliamentary oversights on key thematic issues, 5 Oversights Support to FP Committees for Parliamentary oversights on key thematic issues, 5 Oversights Support the PAs for the legislative assessment of laws in 3 PAs Support the FP Committees for Post-Legislative Scrutiny of some crucial laws, 2 PLS Organize 5 policy dialogues on SDGs Support for speakers and deputy speakers meet for sharing their experiences, 1 Meet Support secretariats for capacity development of their staffs, 100 staff Support women MPs and MPs from disadvantaged groups to enhance their effectiveness through parliamentary debates on different thematic issues, 8 events Support parliamentary reporters for effective Parliament Outreach, 85 journalists Facilitate public-parliament dialogues, public hearing including media interactions in provinces and federal through 20 episodes of radio programmes. Support to engage MPs on advocacy against GBV 1 event Support parliaments to interact with youth, engage media and youth MPs; 6 events PSP final evaluation

A total of 52 MPs of 7 PACs of seven PAs discussed major highlights and observations of the 59th report of the Auditor General and discussed the process of follow-up to settle arrears. LJHRC reviewed 2 bills: Bills to amend some Acts on Sexual Violence 2022 and Bills to amend some Acts on Criminal Offences and Criminal Procedures with PSPs support 5 bills reviewed and consulted (Technical Education and Vocational Training Council Bill; Madarsa Education Board Bill Girl child protection bill; Amendment bill for the election conduction, management, and integration of related laws; Bill on coordination between the province and local levels and dispute management 8 oversights were completed (Oversight of Flood Affected areas and status of services; oversight of road construction, river embankments; oversight of forest encroachment; budget and policy oversights, legislative oversights etc) PSP supported the Law, Justice and Human Rights Committee for 7 oversight missions to review the status of jails in 8 districts of 3 PAs. The legislative assessment of 20 Acts of 4 PAs (PA 1, Madhesh, Bagmati, Lumbini) completed, which provided information on the quality of laws formulated in 4 PAs. The assessment has covered Acts in the areas of GESI, human rights, Climate change, environment and anti-corruption and good governance The Law, Justice and Human Rights Committee of FP discussed on research report and provided recommendations to the Ministry. The Legislative Management Committee of the National Assembly finalized the PLS report of the “Public Procurement Act, 2063 committee and issued 38-point recommendations to the government. 8 policy dialogues on budget allocation and spending on SDGs agendas on climate action” ,“gender equality , natural disaster and Nepal government’s DRR/M plan and strategies , disaster preparedness , good governance and SDG, etc. is completed. 1 meet on inter-legislative interaction between Speakers and Deputy Speakers concluded with a follow-up of previous meets’ towards strengthening the cooperation and coordination among legislative bodies and help ensuring consistency in parliamentary practice. Over 160 staff(23% women) participated in a series of 28 capacity-building events on verbatim management, GESI, Parliamentary Report Writing, Photography-Videography, Website Management, Library Management etc. 8 interactions completed ( 4 Interactions between experienced women leaders of the FP and the women MPs and potential women leaders of and 4 events on Dialogues on Increasing the Candidacy of Women and Marginalized Groups in the Upcoming Election) Over 140 journalists oriented through series of training including budget reporting , climate reporting , disaster affairs reporting , and Experience and Challenges of Parliamentary Journalism in PAs , and “Parliamentary Reporting beyond National Frontiers: experiences from India and Nepal” etc. Besides this, the Journalists Society for Parliamentary Affairs of Lumbini Province coordinated and documented the best parliamentary practices of all 7 PAs. 20 episodes of a radio program via 150 plus radios aired on different thematic issues such as good governance in PAs, the status of bills in parliaments, gender equality, elections, youths, climate change, human rights, parliament affairs etc. and reached out to over 500000 audiences via social media platforms 8 events/programs organized. 3 PAs marked international womens day and conducted 3 interactions on the Issues and challenges of the women migrant workers. 5 radio programs with a focus on discourse on Dignified Menstruation aired between 25 Nov-10 Dec to mark 16 days of activism against Gender-based Violence 6 events on MPs interaction with Youth/ PA day, Open Parliament Week and youth participation, theatre program three transit, radio program on youth participation in parliament, youth and electoral democracy, etc organized that reached out to over 1 million audiences through social platforms. Final Evaluation of project completed

description.narrative:
Disaster risk management modules included in the regular national staff training package.
Number of municipalities having automated building permit system.

comment.narrative:
As of 2012, disaster risk management is not part of the regular national training programme for Government staffs.
As of 2012, the building permit process in all municipalities in Nepal is paper-based.
1. Localization of the DRRM Policy and Strategic Action Plan at the Provincial level. 2. Disaster Information Management System (DIMS) operational and disaster data updated

UNDP CDRMP has been instrumental in assisting all seven provincial governments under leadership of respective Ministry of Internal Affairs and Law (MoIAL) with participation of all Ministries at province level and stakeholders, in formulating their DRR Policy and Strategic Action Plans, in line with national level DRR Policy and Strategic Action Plans. The same has been delivered at local government levels in some 16 paalikas across the country as a pilot intervention, to be rolled out to all 753 LGs over the years. Likewise, additional five paalikas were also assisted for coming up with a total of 15 ward level local disaster and climate risk management plans. The Policy and Strategic Action Plan formulation process has been instrumental in initiating risk informed development planning in the provinces and paalikas.

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

description.narrative: 1.1 NHRC bill passed/amended by the parliament guaranteeing its full independence in line with the Constitution and the Paris Principles. (Independence here will mainly mean the financial independence). 1.2 % of increase of cases resolved vs # of cases backlogged in NHRC 1.3 % of increase of cases resolved vs # of cases registered with NHRC 1.4 % of increase of complaints resolved vs # of complaints filed with NHRC

comment.narrative: N/A

1. At least 75 NHRC staffs are trained and develop their capacity to link SDG and Human Rights. 2. At least 50 law enforcement officials trained on Human Rights. 3. UPR report developed. 4. National conference on business and human rights conducted

• Conducted gender equality assessment at NHRC and developed institutional gender policies at NHRC accompanying an implementation plan which supported NHRC in developing affirmative action on GESI to better institutionalize its work on gender equality and social inclusion. • Trained a total of 181 conflict affect victims including 110 women of seven provinces on psychosocial counselling and mental health that geared up collaboration of victims to raise their voice in advocacy and lobby underscoring the issue of mental health; provided the opportunity to conflict affected group and people discuss more on key issues, challenges and opportunities related to justice to transitional justice. • Substantively digitalized NHRCs paper-based approach in complaint handling by developing online portal, SMS system, and upgrading these digital systems to replace paper-based complaint receiving into new innovation by developing NHRCs mobile app and setting up video conferencing system at its central, provincial and branch offices. • Fielded NHRCs strong collaboration, coordination and leveraged its work among stakeholders, human rights academician, human rights defenders, parliamentarians, and international NHRIs through development and dissemination of ten of human rights promotional materials, human situation report and training manual to media persons and journalists. • Excelled NHRCs outreach in raising public awareness, human rights monitoring and reporting during the pandemic to ensure citizens human rights by conducting human rights response against Ongoing Pandemic COVID-19 through reviewing laws related to crisis and disaster from social security and human rights perspective, broadcasting human rights PSA through local 34 FM stations in 14 local language, and supporting NHRCs human rights monitoring network in 77 district in collaboration with CSOs.
- A study on the coordination among all the commission and draft the strategy - UPR implementation strategy developed - Training module on human trafficking and migrant workers

- Completed a study on the coordination strategy of the commission and discussed amongst all the commissions - Draft strategy on UPR is already in place but requires further discussion with concerned government agencies - Developed a training manual on migrant workers and human trafficking and tested among the NHRC officials and other experts working in this area.

- Project document for Parliament Support Project developed, submitted and got approval from Norway - National and sub-national level consultations were conducted with government, civil society, implementing partners, Think tanks for formulation of new CPD - New CPD 2023-2027 was formulated and submitted to EB for approval in February session

description.narrative: 1. # of staff recruited in Phase I 2. # of staff recruited in Phase II 3. # of staff recruited in Phase III

comment.narrative: 1. 109 staffs will be on board and mobilized 2. Provide TA to MoFAGA and other line ministries as well as government agencies and PLGs

- 16 laws and regulations and procedures related to the Provincial Center for Good Governance (PCGG) and standards of the organizational structures and positions of employees and also rule to manage the Local Development Regional Training Center were drafted through experts mobilization - Provided technical support to the MoFAGA to develop comprehensive planning, monitoring, and oversight system to be adopted by the provincial and local governments (PLGs). In order to operate the guideline, a computer-based software has been developed which will help PLGS to follow the guidelines and produce reports efficiently. - Provided technical support to the Ministry of Law, Justice and Parliamentary Affairs (MPLJPA), National Human Rights Commission (NHRC), Parliament/ Province Assembly in reviewing different 14 legislations related to gender based violence (GBV), rape, discriminations based on caste and persons with disability, pandemic and disaster management, delegated legislation in particular on land and other draft bills on tourism. - Conducted orientation to the 326 (165 women) of the members of province assembly on law drafting and making process in the provinces. - Provided technical support to the Election Commission of Nepal to draft three policies on Human Resource Development (HRD), Social Media and Civic and Voter Education. - provided technical support to the MoFAGA to revise and approve the Local Institution Self-Assessment (LISA) tool which is now in place to roll to the local government level - Provided technical expertise in developing a reporting software called SUTRA for the provincial and local governance to enhance accountable system to track the financial administration. - technical and financial support provided to the Right to Information Commission in Nepal to develop right to information action plan.
1. Seven Provincial Centers for Good Governance (PCGGs) are functional in all the provinces 2. Baseline Survey conducted 3. Operational Guidelines for the Innovative Partnership Fund (IPF) finalized

-All the seven PCGGs are operational and have been providing training to civil servants and elected representatives in their provinces. -Conducted baseline survey covering the composite of three different elements included service recipients’ experiences of public service provided by the LGs, competency of elected representatives at LGs, and participation of LG executive council members from women and marginalized communities in LG affairs. -Finalized IPF guideline and rolled out in all the seven provinces

description.narrative: - Pilot-Province ‘Data and M&E-Reporting’ systems and procedures are functional and design is more inclusive than at the start of the project and compared to other Provinces - Pilot-Province PCGG has a long-term Organisational strategy for becoming a state of the art resource/training centre, including training strategy and approach for strengthening inclusiveness of PLG decision & policy making, GRID and LG basic service delivery - Number of published policy papers of each (i) Municipality Association of Nepal (MuAN), and (ii) Nepal Association of Rural Municipalities in Nepal (NARMIN) on LG decision-making and BSD, with specific focus on LG capacity for and responsiveness to inclusive ways of working, GESI objectives and GRID.

comment.narrative: - TBD - PCGG Organisation Strategy includes no or limited GRID and GESI objectives and targeted CD activities - xx% of Women and Dalit-women ward members and LG staff (intended beneficiaries of PCGG services), which perceive PCGG strategy and services as adequately systemically inclusive - xx number of published policy papers over 2018-2021
N.A.
N.A.

1. Complete the recruitment of all TA staff 2. Conduct orientation to the thematic experts of TA staff of Provincial and Local Governance and Support Programme 3. Support to draft at least 6 bills, procedures and system, and 4. Conduct the mapping of donors and contributors in the area of governance, federalism in Nepal

1. All staff 109 staff are fully on board. 2. Conducted thematic and issue based training for approx 80 staff (TA). 3. Supported the Ministry of Federal Affairs and General Administration to draft about 9 bills. and 4. Conducted and finalized a report on mapping of all the development partners in the area of governance.

- Mid-term review (MTR) of the Provincial and Local Governance Support and Programme (PLGSP) conducted to inform government partners. - Third Party Monitoring (TPM) conducted to provide quality assurance for the smooth implementation of PLGSP - Retention strategy drafted and implemented - Conduct training on Gender and Social Inclusion (GESI) for the TA and Government officials - Conduct training to the capacity development officials of PLGSP and Government staffers on curricula development, needs assessment etc. - Conduct Need Assessment of Provincial Center for Good Governance (PCGG) - Data and M&E-Reporting’ systems and procedures produce required data and reports 50% more dis-aggregated data available for GESI and 50% more GESI indicators in LG progress reports - Conduct the Mapping of the Basic Service Delivery (BSD) in selected local bodies - PCGG Strategy includes an holistic approach and Plan to deliver GRID and GESI responsive CD services for strengthening PLG system inclusiveness and achieving PLG GESI objectives, including specific GESI trainings
- Training outcome monitoring system in place for continuous learning and adaptation on GRID and GESI CD approach and interventions
- 100% increase in % of 1) PCGG and 2) PLG staff, which perceive PCGG strategy and services as inclusive - Assessment of PCGG existing strategy and training, PCGG and PLG staff perception survey on inclusiveness of PCGG strategy and services
- Minimum #3 or 100% increase of publications by MuAN and Minimum #2 or 100% increase of publications by NARMIN - Conduct GESi training for the officials of local bodies

- Supported to finalize the MTR report. The report has been submitted to the Ministry of Federal Affairs and General Affairs (MOFAGA) and National Executive Committee (NEC) as well as National Steering Committee (NSC) meeting. - Around oriented and inducted 45 Government of Nepal (GON) and TA staff on GESI - Around 45 Capacity Development staff and government officials from provinces trained - Finalized draft strategy for retention of TA staff. - Completed orientation on Project Database Portal (PDP) development and conducted the orientation to the 23 municipalities - Finalized the inception report of Basic Service Delivery (BSD) by selecting third party to conduct it. The inception report is being discussed. - Finalized the comparative analysis of three PCGG and report is being analyzed to develop the strategy - Conducted GESI training for around 50 officials of local bodies and resolutions passed.

Technical and extension support to 37 Palikas through LOA Technical and Management training to Cooperatives Improve physical facility of the post-harvest technology at NARC Provide post harvest training to public extension officer Conduct Project Midterm review South South Cooperation

• 37 Palika received technical and extension support to palikas • 4 cooperatives received management training and 10 cooperatives/market centers received cooperative management and market management software • One NARC HRD postharvest lab received physical facility improvement support • 3 slots of postharvest training to lead farmers (NARC) • TOR finalize and share with UNDP for initiate process of mid-term evaluation in 2021 • South-south cooperation (visit to Myanmar for exchange knowledge) is postponed because of COVID Pandemic

• Technical and extension support to 37 Palikas through LOA (37 palikas) • 10 Technical and Management training to Cooperatives and 10 cooperatives will received management and market support (10) • Improve physical facility of the post-harvest technology at NARC (1) • Provide post-harvest training to public extension officer (10) • Conduct Project Midterm review (1) • Partnership with FM station for agir information and technology dissemination(3) • Agriculture learning center estabilished (14)

Technical and extension support provided to 37 Palikas • Six Technical and Management training organized for Cooperatives and 24 Coop received management and market support • One Physical facility supported to NARC for the post-harvest technology • 12 production and post-harvest training conducted for the public extension officer • Conducted Project Midterm review (1) • Partnership was done with 3 FM radio station for agir information and technology dissemination • 14 Agriculture learning center established

• Technical and extension support to 37 Palikas through LOA (37 palikas)
• Training to 345 Palika technicians, lead farmers, cooperative operators and farmers
• 5 Technical studies conducted
• 2 Exposure visits for MoALD and NARC
• Exposure visits for Palika technicians, lead farmers and cooperatives (tentatively 112 pax)
Conduct final evaluation of project

- Provided continuous support and technical guidance to 37 Local Governments and cooperatives for effective implementation of planned activities
345 technicians, cooperative members, and farmers received training on production, plant clinics, soil and water management, postharvest loss management orientation, agricultural market management, farm record keeping
5 technical studies conducted; Financial incentive study, value chain study, GESI study, livelihood and returnee migrant assessment study, and packhouse study
- Two Exposure visit conducted; 1) MOALD Secretary, VCDP Chair, NPD, NPM and focal persons of KOICA and UNDP paid a visit to Thailand and South Korea. It was to learn agricultural practices of neighboring countries and explore South South North cooperation opportunities; 2) 22 Agriculture Technicians (4 female, 7 from minority groups) visited India to gain understanding on India’s extension system, postharvest technologies and cooperative management practices
112 Palika technicians, lead farmers and cooperative operators joined the exposure visits in domestic sites
Conducted Final Evaluation of project

comment.narrative: 1. Construct (6) cooperative markets at district and municipal level 2. Install (6) cooling chamber at collection centers built 3. Support (13) cooperatives and market centers with transportation vehicle support 4. 15,000 farmers benefitted from marketing facilities support.

4 Agreements (LoAs and RPA) signed and work is undergoing 8 Cooling Chambers installed Procurement of 25 Vehicles is in the process A total of 19,331 farmers (female-9931; male-9400) benefitted from marketing facilities support. As a result, they were able to sold 8,590.20 Tons of Fruits and Vegetables worth NPR 193,668,913.50.

1. Continue the construction of Marketing outlets in Chitwan, Kavre, Nuwakot, Ratnanagar and Bennighat Rorang = #5 2. Cold room installation for Marketing outlets in Chitwan, Kavre, Nuwakot, Ratnanagar and Benighat Rorang = #4 3. Input support (Crates) to Cooperatives and DCUs =#6 (districts) 4. Support Business Plan Development of (18) primary cooperatives and Market outlets of Chabahil and Benighat Rorang = # 20 5. Mid Term Review of CMDP = # 1 6. Operation of additional Markets (Benighat Rorang) = #1 7. Piloting a model intervention to promote diversification of agriculture products for improving livelihoods in CMDP project districts (within and adjoining CMDP municipalities) and link to the CMDP market

1. Marketing outlet in Nuwakot and Benighat is in last stage of construction. Remaining 3 marketing outlets construction is ongoing 2. Installed 8 cold room for 4 primary cooperatives 3. 6,960 crates supported to cooperatives and DCUs 4. Supported for preparation of 20 business plan 5. Mid term review completed 6. Benighat Rorang market is in operation 7. The project has supported for the construction of marketing outlets for dairy products in Chitwan and Makwanpur districts

• Organized 1 networking event • Development of 20 Business Plan • Conduct 3 studies • Construct 9 cooperative market • Conduct 2 studies related to cooperative and cooperative market • Organize 2 international level exposure visit for learning and knowledge sharing as per the target of 2. • Organize 2 national level exposure visit for learning and knowledge sharing as per the target of 2. • Provide input support to 3000 farmers.

• Organized 1 networking event to create linkage between cooperative market and cooperatives as planned. • 20 Business Plan Developed against the target of 20. • Conducted 3 end program studies as planned (endline, exit strategy, documentation of learning and documentation). • Constructed 9 cooperative market as planned (1 Satellite, 1 Regional, 3 district and 4 municipal) . • Conducted 2 studies related to cooperative and cooperative market as planned (2). • Organized 2 international level exposure visit for learning and knowledge sharing as per the target of 2. • Organized 2 national level exposure visit for learning and knowledge sharing as per the target of 2. • Provided input support benefitting 3,927 smallholder farmers against the target of 3000 farmers.

comment.narrative: Zero Zero Zero Zero Zero Zero Zero Zero Zero Zero, Zero, Not in place, Not in place, Not in place Zero

• Completion of 120 schemes including 30 small-scale tourism infrastructure
• Creation of Short term employment for 1600 vulnerable tourism sector workers
• Leverage 15 % co-financing from local government and private sector tourism association
• Development three strategy and guideless

• Completed 124 Schemes including 36 small-scale tourism infrastructure
• Short term employment created for 5,645 vulnerable tourism sector workers and created 76,948 person-days of total employment. Out of 5645 tourism workers, 1442 (25.5%) women were provided 21,445 person-days of employment.
• Project leveraged 18% co-financing from local government and private sector tourism associations
• Project support to develop National Tourism Recovery Strategy document, and two tourism industry-level service standards for “Food and Beverage Service Standards” for Restaurant and Bars in coordination with REBAN, and “Tourist Vehicle Operating Standards Guidelines” in coordination with Nepal Tourist Vehicle Association (NTVA).

45 Number of small-scale tourism infrastructure built, renovated and reconstructed
Skill enhancement of 400 tourism workers
Five number of guidelines/tools developed on standardization for tourism services
Policy intervention for domestic tourism promotion

Constructed 64 small scale tourism infrastructure in collaboration with 29 R/Municipalities. Project created estimated 20,000-man days of short-term employment while construction of small-scale tourism infrastructure in collaboration with local government
Provided capacity building training to around 1287 tourism sector workforce; out of which 38% were women.
Prepared 6 tourism service standards and guidelines
1 national tourism recovery strategy developed.
Accomplished 7 province level domestic tourism strategic action plan preparation workshop

description.narrative: 223 proportional rep. from each sector

comment.narrative: 119 Women, 10 Dalit, 2 PWD and 0 LGBTIQ+ mobilized to reach out community through Clinical Legal Education and legal internship program

- 110 of law graduates from marginalized communities to empower women, Dalit, PWD, LGBTIQ+ and other marginalized communities with targeted interventions - 8 draft bills drafted for the province governments in Nepal - 150,000 (33% women) persons benefited from legal aid services in four provinces - One study on access to justice of women and marginalized groups conducted - Around 100 officials of province government trained on law making process - Around 200 officials of judicial committees under local bodies trained on their roles/responsibilities and process/procedures of dispute settlement - Prepare the concept note and positions paper on Business and Human Rights Action Plan in a consultative manner - Establish Hello Chief Minister and Namaste Mayor in two provinces.

- Benefited around 100 students and 30 women and lawyers from marginalized from clinical law education and law internship programme. - Supported to draft 6 bills in Karnali Province - Benefited around 25,840 persons (40% women) from legal aid services. - Conducted the assessment on access to justice of Women and Vulnerable Communities in numbers of provinces - Oriented 110 officials of province and local government in sub-national level. - Oriented around 230 officials of judicial committee on mandates of judicial committees, dispute resolution methods and mediation. - Drafted a comprehensive position paper on business and human rights in Nepal. - Established Hello Chief Minister and Namaste Mayors in two provinces namely Karnali and Sudurpaschim Province.

1. Support to the GoN in producing key policy document (13) such as LDC graduation strategy, Leave No One Behind national framework, national strategy of industrial sector to promote domestic production and consumption, national action plan against suicide, national human resource plan, provincial level climate change financing framework.
2. Provided capacity building training on M&E for more than 70 government officials, national account and macroeconomic perspectives for 49 GoN officials to enhance their understanding on these issues.
3. Supported NPC in orienting SDG at sub-national levels- more than 1,700 officials trained on SDG aligned planning, SDG monitoring and its reporting. The events held across 7 provinces and 753 local governments
4. Supported to develop and roll out MTEF software for the provinces in order to improve the planning process aligned with the SDGs.

description.narrative:
1. Number of people reached through Information, Education and Communication on DRR 2. % of population benefitted through Hazard and risk assessment mapping and impact modelling 3. % increase of population and authorities with enhanced understanding of earthquake and urban risks
4. Number of people covered by early action/contingency plans 5. Number of municipal and provincial EOCs operational with minimal functional benchmark 6. Number of Provincial Earthquake Response Framework (PERF) formulated based on the earthquake risk scenario and anticipated
7. Inter-provincial and inter-municipal coordination mechanism established and formalized 8. Number of cluster members trained to integrate protection issues in the cluster contingency plans 9. Number of provincial level key clusters specific contingency plans prepared for effective response
10. Number of community small-scale infrastructures and facilities built or protected 11. Number of community volunteers, as CERT members, trained and equipped on life saving response skills and readiness 12. Community readiness attested and ensured through conduct of number of emergency simulation exercises 13. Number of security forces trained on technological solutions 14. Number of demonstration houses retrofitted to illustrate technical solution for replication
15. Number of Shared Learning Dialogues with provincial, local level and private sectors

sector.narrative: Structural transformation, including green, inclusive and digital transitions

condition.narrative: UNDP as the Implementing Partner will comply with the policies, procedures and practices of the United Nations Security Management System (UNSMS.)

description.narrative:
1.1 Number of new data sources used at Country Office level.
1.2 Diversity of different data sources used
1.3 Number of sustainable development solutions identified and documented
1.4 Variety of innovation methods used to test and iterate over the different hypotheses.
2.1 Learning the Accelerator Lab Network shares with a public audience (outside of UNDP)
2.2 Number of lab insights, processes or products which are taken up by governments or other partners
2.3 Proportion of partners and governments that increase their take up of the innovation methods included in the Accelerator Lab Network suite
2.4 Proportion of UNDP Country Office programming influenced by the Accelerator Lab Network’s work and methodologies
3.1 Size and density of the network effects among the Accelerator Labs
3.2 Number of new models, approaches, and insights documented and disseminated throughout the network.
3.3 Engagement of the broader Development ecosystem with published learnings from the Accelerator Lab Network.
3.4 Amount of influence the learnings and methods of the Accelerator Lab Networks have on the discourse of the broader Development ecosystem.

comment.narrative: Not available

Target 1.1 5 new data sources per year Target 1.2: 6 different type of data sources per year Target 1.3: 35 new potential solutions per lab per year Target 1.4: 5 innovation methods per Action Plan. Target 2.1: At least 6 blogs or other (substantial) media posts published per year. Target 2.2: At least 50% of our learning challenges have the public sector or other partner as counterpart. Target 2.3: At least one government entity or partner has adopted an accelerator lab method. Target 2.4: At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects. Target 3.2: 25% of information shared across the network should be methodology, or sustainable development issue and opportunity related. Target 3.3.1: The Accelerator Lab Networks work is featured at least 3 times per month in Country Office Social Media channels and tag UNDP Accelerator Labs accounts. Target: 3.3.2: The Lab team engages at least once a month across any social media conversation related to the work of the Accelerator Lab Network, from either personal or CO account. Target 3.4.1: Connect the Accelerator Lab’s publications to local development ecosystem through social media. Target 3.4.2: Identify and engage with 5 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader Development Ecosystem.

Result 1.1. 1 new data source per year used in 2020 (Qualitative data such as narrative stories and anecdotes). Result 1.2: 2 different types of data sources per year (Geospatial data and Big Data). Result 1.3: 15 new potential solutions per year entered in the solution mapping platform ranging from solutions to tackle solid waste management, creating employment, COVID response and relief etc. Result 1.4: 3 innovation methods per action plan (ranging from pilots, collective intelligence, prototyping, behavioral change communications, surveys, solutions mapping among others). Result 2.1: 10 blogs published per year along with a video on pocket park experiment. Result 2.2: At least 50% of our learning challenges have the public sector or other partners as counterparts. Result 2.3: Yes, one. Collective Intelligence used for Kathmandu Business Hub by UNDP along with Local Government. Result 2.4: At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects related waste management and unemployment. Result 3.2: 50% of the information shared at both global and regional platforms. Acclab Nepal has also collaborated separately with 5 other Labs for experience sharing. Result 3.3.1 : The work of AccLab is featured 4 times a month on an average. Result 3.3.2: The lab team engages at least five times a month on an average. Result 3.4.1: 2 publications of the Accelerator Lab connected to local development ecosystem through social media. Result 3.4.2: Identified and Engaged with 5 new relevant local social media profiles of the broader development ecosystem.

Target 1.4: 10 innovation methods per Action Plan.

(RESULTS ACHIEVED AS OF December 2021)
Result 1.1: 7 new data sources used in 2021 (primary, secondary, open data, surveys, mobile data, GIS, narratives)

Result 1.3: 10+ new potential solutions identified (plastic waste management, urban mobility, COVID relief and/or recovery, green technology etc.)

Result 2.1: A total of 9 blogs published and 2 research reports on plastic waste management and Digital Socio-Economic Assessment.
Result 2.2: At least 50% of our learning challenges have public sector and local governments involved as counterparts.
Result: 2.3: A total of 2 government entities have adopted our methods. 1. Pokhara Metropolitan City Office adopted the innovative methodology of testing plastic bricks; 2. Ministry of Land Management, Cooperatives and Poverty Alleviation approved and initiated the digital marketing platform for farmers cooperative markets.
Result 2.4: At least 50% of our learning challenges are providing actionable knowledge to ongoing CO projects such as the plastic waste management research is supporting the Promoting Green Recovery Project (PGRP) and digital marketing platform is being developed together with Cooperative Market Development Programme (CMDP).
Results 3.2: 20% of information shared across the network is related to methodology/ SDG issues and opportunities such as solid waste management, innovation, digital transformation, decent work and employment etc.

description.narrative: # of waste management system established in municipality

comment.narrative: At least in 2 municipalities

• Agreement for Partnership on municipal waste management is establishment with two municipalities: Dhankuta: • 1,400 HHs are expected to benefit in Dhankuta municipality • Waste segregation plant platform is initiated • School level and community level orientation is completed • Waste bins procurement is initiated • Exposure visit is taken a place to Guwahati, Asam, India for the municipal authorities for the best practices in municipal waste management. Ghorahi Sub-metropolitan City: • LOA is made with the municipality • As private sector is already building a huge waste management plant, UNDP support is expected to benefit for 1,550 HHs for household biogas facility • Need assessment of 8 hospitals carried out with the close consultation with Department of Health Services (DoHS)/Ministry of Health and Population, further collaboration is being done with selected 4 hospitals for the medical waste management is being taken a place • Partnership agreement is established with 2 local governments (Karjanha Municipality and Lumbini Buddhist university/Devdaha Municipality) • Agroforest and multilayer plantation in 70 Hectare (60 hectare in Karjanha and 10 hectares in Devdaha/LBU) of degraded and fallow land • Selection of 343 beneficiary HHs, (of which 60% women 44% beneficiaries are from Dalit community and is provisioned)

comment.narrative: - Three Provinces have finalized the SDG platform Themes and potential organization (Province 2 on Resilient Cities-Extraction of Natural Resources, Gandaki Province- Green Finance and Karnalai Province- Food Security) are finalized - 11 local governments prepared the periodic plan and start implementing - UNDP has developed and rolled out a dedicated socio-economic and livelihood recovery package. The package program aims to increase the basic income of vulnerable groups by promoting local resource-based enterprises- on farm based and off farmed. - 12,386 households were directly benefitted from socio-economic recovery interventions to improve their income and livelihood - 18 socio-economic recovery project in partnerships with municipalities implemented.
- At least Three SDG platforms established and functional - 5000 new green entrepreneurs will be established

- One SDG platform on Food Security was established in Karnali province. The MOU was signed with Mid-Western University. - 5,602 new ‘green’ entrepreneurs (2,871 women) were trained and created, and support continued to 3,178 existing ones (1,629 women), as a result of UNDP’s partnership with 13 municipalities

description.narrative: Frontline health workers are better protected against Covid-19

comment.narrative: zero
Support provincial governments in better management of COVID-19 response

Nepal’s health workers are now better protected against COVID-19 and prepared to manage the outbreak through direct support from the UNDP Nepal CO and Field Offices. In total, over 165,000 surgical and cloth masks have been provided to frontline responders through local governments. A new Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) testing machine, with the capacity of analysing more than 200 tests per day was installed in Lumbini Provincial Hospital of Province 5. The public awareness of COVID-19, immediate protection measures, and short-term response and recovery efforts has been significantly enhanced. In total, over 3 million people have been reached through radio, public service announcements (PSAs) and social media, in an effort to assist in resolving health, social and economic issues during the crisis. More than 1,500 people also received direct support through call-in radio programmes, with 30 per cent of issues resolved. Some examples included ensuring that immediate relief support reached the most vulnerable groups, connecting farmers with buyers, and providing medical advice to pregnant mothers. With support from UNDP, UNFPA and UN Women, The Association of Community Radio Broadcasters (ACORAB) and its Community Information Network (CIN), hosted the popular radio show ‘Jeevan Rakshya’ to ensure continued connectivity and access to relevant authorities and support services, despite mobility restrictions during the nation-wide lockdown.
- Provide health system support to federal and sub-national governments as per need and request

UNDP Nepal’s intervention contributes to strengthen the health system of Nepal and the efforts of government of Nepal to contain the virus. UNDP supported the federal, provincial and local government by procuring the much needed emergency medical supplies including the oxygen concentrators, hospital beds, protective gears as per the request from the government. - In 2021, UNDP handed over 400 oxygen concentrators to critical health care facilities across all the seven provinces in response to the request from the Ministry of Health and Population to meet the immediate medical oxygen gap during the second wave. - 22,000 antigen test kits provided to local authorities to improve the screening of people entering Nepal from different point of entry. - About 15,000 + individuals living in the isolation were provided with needful guidance, referral to doctors, emergency support, linking with ambulances, consultation with doctors. 1,800+ migrant returnees particularly women, children and elderly were provided support by transporting them from point of entry (border) to the holding centres. - Public awareness of COVID-19, immediate protection measures, and short-term response and recovery efforts has been significantly enhanced. Over 3.5 million people have been reached through second phase of the live phone-in radio programme, public service announcements (PSAs) and social media, in an effort to assist in resolving health, social and economic issues during the crisis. - Over 500 direct calls and queries of listeners were received and addressed by connecting them live with 55 concerned government authorities and relevant stakeholders.

description.narrative: Indicator 1. Number of municipalities with data-informed climate responsive policies, plans and institutions in place to reduce the physical and economic losses from climate induced disasters and strengthen social cohesion (Strategic Plan Output Indicator 2.3.1.2). Indicator 2. Number of households, municipality and rural municipality benefited from project activities. Indicator 3. Number of policies, guidelines and plans developed and strengthened to identify and integrate climate change adaptation strategies and measures. Indicator 4. Institutional arrangements to lead, coordinate and support the integration of climate change adaptation into relevant policies, plans and associated processes Indicator 5. Types and extent of assets strengthened and/or better managed to withstand the effects of climate change Indicator 6. Extent of adoption of climate-resilient technologies/practices

comment.narrative: 1. No municipalities with data-informed climate responsive policies, plans and institutions 2. No IWM Policy with guidelines for gender mainstreaming, harmonised watershed prioritization tool, climate resilience guidelines for infrastructure development 3. No cross-institutional platforms for IWM 4. Insufficient hydrometric equipment to monitor climate variables in the target watersheds 5. 30% of waters sources dried up 6. No Non-climate resilient land use practices leading to land degradation
1. Project document signed. 2. Governance mechanism established.
The project was finally signed in December 2020. The project will accelerate its implementation from 2021.

1. Inception workshop organized 2. PPG stage baseline for the project area revised. 3. Multi-hazard risk and vulnerability mapping/modelling prepared 4. Watershed condition methodologies and guidelines prepared and shared. 5. National institutional guidelines, standards and data sharing protocols for IWM prepared. 6. Integrated watershed plan for Lower Dudh Koshi watershed prepared 7. Stock take of traditional practices and approaches of Majhi community 8. NRM Groups identified and capacity assessed. 9. 25 water sources protected and10 multiple-use ponds constructed. 10. 1 inter-disciplinary coordination mechanism setup at the Local Level 11. 121,606 household directly benefitted from nature based solutions

- National and Local Level Inception workshop organized - 4 Inter-disciplinary coordination setup at Local Level - 3 water sources protected and 2 catchment ponds constructed - 814 HHs directly benefitted from nature based solutions.

1. Multi-hazard risk and vulnerability mapping/modelling prepared 2. Watershed condition methodologies and guidelines prepared and shared. 3. 8 data-informed climate responsive local level plans prepared 4. 8 inter-disciplinary coordination mechanisms at the local level 5. 6 policies, guidelines and tools developed on Watershed Management 6. 6 Number of Multi-institutional IWM coordination platforms established at central, provincial and local levels 7. 2 hydrological stations installed 8. 2 meteorological stations installed 9. 80 catchment ponds constructed 10. 50km of contour trench constructed 11. 25 Water holes constructed 12. 700 water sources protected/rehabilitated 13. Conservation farming adopted on 37.63% of all agricultural land) 3763 ha 14. Establishment of water use/reuse system (Rain water harvesting, household roof to root water harvesting) on 1000 ha 15. Drought resistant crop variety promoted on 10% of drought affected land (20,000 ha). 17. At least 1250 Farmers trained on conservation farming and agroforestry 18. 10 persons trained in construct fuel efficient stoves & fuel efficient stoves provided to 2500 households 19. 800 NRM groups strengthened and 150 NRM groups operational plans revised

description.narrative: 1.1 Number of local governments with Vulnerability information management system (VIMS) developed and used to identify the vulnerable people 1.2 No. of municipalities/rural municipalities that conducted Vulnerability assessment using revised indicators to identify vulnerable women in their location 1.3 No. of local governments with policies, guidelines and frameworks related to social protection schemes in place and implemented

comment.narrative: 1. Develop 2 QR code-based Socio-Economic Vulnerability Information Management Systems to support Local government to allow them to identify vulnerable people/communities for subsequent response to their immediate needs and build access to basic and essential services 2. Conduct socio-economic vulnerability assessment in 2 new project locations in Panchapuri Municipality of Surkhet and Simkot Rural Municipality of Humla in Karnali province 3. Conduct project review and planning workshop

1. 2 QR Code based SEVIMS has been developed and finalized after rounds of consultations with municipal authorities and field testing to incorporate practical needs. The SEVIMS is ready to handover to respective local governments of Beni Municipality and Putalibazaar Municipality in Gandaki Province. The municipality has also initiated to collect required data and govt server to host the system live and sustain the initiative. 2. A detailed socio-economic vulnerability assessment has been conducted and completed by Mid-West University in 2 local government level- Panchapuri Municipality in Surkhet District and Simkot Rural Municipality in Humla district. Total of 980 vulnerable women has been identified in Panchapuri Municipality and 531 in Simikot Rural Municipality. The timely assessment has allowed project to identify actual 1000 beneficiaries to provide support to them. 3. A 2 days residential workshop was conducted in Chitwan together with representatives of 4 local governments, private sector, 5 NGO partners, 1 govt bank, 2 universities and UNDP team. The workshop reviewed the progress made in 2022 and noted that government and stakeholders have recommended to upscale the project with further commitments and collaboration opportunities by local government.

title.narrative: Renewable Energy for Resilient Agri-Food Systems (RERAS)

description.narrative: The project aims to increase agricultural productivity and food and nutrition security by enhancing the use of reliable and affordable energy solutions for production, irrigation, processing, storing, packaging, and marketing of locally grown farm products. The project will support establishing dedicated funds at local level to support the farmers for use of modern technology in production and processing. The project will work with local and provincial governments, cooperatives, traders, and service providers to enhance their knowledge on use of modern technology for food production and sustainable food system management through capacity building and provisions for climate information, policies, finance, and farm research. Extensive use of energy-based and climate smart technology will help reduce the cost of production and increase local food supply. This will also support economic empowerment of women and marginalized groups through job creation and contribute to environment.The project will be implemented

participating-org.narrative: Royal Ministry of Foreign Affair, Norway

title.narrative: Developing Capacity for Enhancing Large-scale Investment in Nepal (DCEL)

description.narrative: The project Developing Capacity for Enhancing Large-scale Investment in Nepal (DCEL) aims to assist the Government of Nepal in closing the financial gap to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and address post-graduation challenges. Central to the projects strategies is the development of institutional capacity, encompassing the establishment of system, creation of tools and standards, and growth of human resources. Key interventions encompass studies, guideline formation, advocacy events, training, and exposure provision. These efforts collectively foster a favorable climate for significant private investments, elevating IBN as a proficient Investment Promotion Agency (IPA) and Public Private Partnership (PPP) Centre of Excellence in Nepal to handhold investors at every stage of investment cycle.

title.narrative: Developing capacity for enhancing large scale investment in Nepal (DCEL)

To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

title.narrative: Green Job Creation through Recycling and Upcycling in Pokhara Metropolitan City (GCRU)

description.narrative: The “Green Job Creation through Recycling and Upcycling in Pokhara Metropolitan City (GCRU) project” is designed to promote the green economy by establishing recycling/upcycling (RU) enterprises based on the circular economy model, in place of the current throw away approach, thereby increasing green jobs and reducing pollution and environmental impact of solid waste. This objective will be achieved through three interlinked areas of interventions – policy and coordination, promoting of RU enterprises, and empowering communities for increased awareness and community level RU initiatives.

Road accidents in Nepal bring heavy losses to lives and to the economy every year. Despite all efforts, casualties have increased by 155% in the last 15 years. Experts estimate that the number may reach 4,500 by 2030, which means 12 people dying on roads every day. Women, children, and people with disability are particularly at high risk of road incidents. The majority of casualties from road crashes are among those of productive age, resulting in an economic loss of USD 122,88 million (equivalent to nearly 1.5% of GDP) in 2017. Aligned to UN General Assembly resolution A/RES/74/299, “Improving global road safety”, with an explicit target of reducing 50% of road traffic deaths and injuries by 2030, Nepal developed the National Road Safety Action Plan (NRSAP) (2021 – 2030) in 2020. However, the implementation of NRSAP lagged due to poor regulations, limited technical capacity, and weak enforcement. The inadequate capacity of the National Road Safety Council (NRSC) to function as the lead agency for road safety

title.narrative: Strengthening E-Governance for Inclusive and Accountable Society

description.narrative: The purpose of the Initiation Plan Document is to outline UNDPs efforts with the E-Governance Commission of Nepal to develop a comprehensive e-governance blueprint. This blueprint aims to utilize ICTs to enhance the efficiency, transparency, and accountability of government operations and services, ultimately building a more efficient, effective, and responsive public sector. The project aligns with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and Nepals national development goals, with a focus on promoting inclusive development and public participation. Under this partnership, UNDP will provide technical assistance and capacity building support to the E-Governance Commission. The collaboration will encompass various activities, including e-governance policy development, stakeholder analysis and engagement, assessment of the current state of e-governance, institutional capacity building, identification of key performance indicators (KPIs), promotion of service delivery through AI powered chatbot system and monit

title.narrative: Circular and Green Economy for Environmental Restoration in Nepal (GREEN)

description.narrative: This Initiation Plan has been developed to achieve national priorities set by the Government of Nepal (15th Development Plan) and UNDP Country Program Document (2023-2027) on environmental management and sustainable livelihoods. This will be attained through different collective methods that includes i) portfolio approach on circularity and green economy; ii) research and development; and iii) co-financing innovative initiatives of Accelerator Lab in Nepal. The Initiation Plan aims to explore integrated approach to create synergy between different programmatic interventions on achieving common goals related to healthy and sustainable future directly contributing to 12 out of 17 SDGs. There are four specific objectives that this initiation plan fosters to structure, to be followed by detail project document after monitoring the progress within the next 18 months. (1). title.narrative: Strengthening Capacity of the Public Service Commission of Nepal

description.narrative: Through this initiation plan, UNDP Nepal aims to support the Public Service Commission of Nepal in strengthening its capacity to upscale and update its work on the Assessment Center Methodology (ACM). By implementing this Plan, UNDP intends to build on its past support through its Project to PREPARE Public Administration for State Reform (2013 - 2017) and its achievements. With UNDP support, the Commission introduced the ACM in 2013-2014. This Initiation Plan will contribute to making Nepal’s public administration more efficient, agile, and results oriented as envisaged by the current 15th Plan of the Government of Nepal and the UNSDCF Outcome 4/UNDP Nepal Country Programme Document (CPD) Outcome 2 on Governance, federalism, participation and inclusion. Specifically, it will contribute to achievement of the CPD Output 2.3: Improved mechanisms for promoting transparency and public participation.The Initiation Plan will be implemented for a maximum of 18 months. Within this time, the aim is also to develop a more comprehensive long-term project for 4-5 years for system strengthening of the Commission. The project will also explore for future partnerships with the seven Province Public Service Commissions (PPSCs) in the full-fledged project and subsequent resource mobilization. Though the PPSCs are autonomous institutions, they closely follow the standards and practices of the Public Service Commission. In the federal context, and in the spirit of the 2015 Constitution, the improved recruitment processes also aim, in the long run, to contribute to proportional representation of women and marginalized groups in state mechanisms.

title.narrative: Advancing Business and Human Rights through supporting implementation of National Plan of Action (Advancing B+HR)

description.narrative: The purpose of the Initiation Plan Document is to outline UNDPs efforts with the Ministry of Labour, Employment and Social Security (MoLESS), private sector, and civil society organizations (CSOs) in Nepal to establish the basic foundation and introduce necessary processes and procedures to advance Business and Human Rights in Nepal, including by widely disseminating the National Plan of Action (NAP) on Business and Human Rights in support of its effective implementation. Through the Initiation Plan, UNDP will also make particular efforts to promote and protect human rights of women and the most marginalized and vulnerable groups in the private sector in Nepal, taking into account specific issues and concerns.

title.narrative: Strengthening Urban Preparedness and Earthquake Response in Western region of Nepal (SUPER-II)

description.narrative: Building on past urban preparedness and drawing learnings from recent disasters, the action aims to institutionalize operational urban and earthquake preparedness and response in targeted provinces and municipalities and link it with federal mechanism. With inclusion at the heart of the action, addressing specific needs of vulnerable groups especially women, children and persons with disabilities, it also intends to upscale interventions in project area and beyond. The key result areas: i)Government-led operational system for urban and earthquake preparedness institutionalized at provincial and local level for effective response ii) Coordination and information management strengthened at provincial and municipal levels, aligned with national framework, for disaster risk governance; iii) Enhanced policy engagement for scalability of disaster preparedness and response mechanism at federal, provincial, and local levels.It has been identified based on deeper risk analysis, consultation with federal, provincial, and municipal governments and development partners engaged in similar interventions, lessons learned from the recent earthquakes including Jajarkot earthquake & learnings from SUPER. Under the leadership of the NDRRMA, provincial government and municipalities, the project will be implemented by a UNDP led consortium with UNICEF and UN WOMEN and HI, and in close coordination with RCO, WHO, PIN, NRCS, CSOs and other development partners and will engage parliamentarians, various association of municipalities, government training institutions, media and private sector. In the ongoing federalization context, the Action aims to institutionalize urban preparedness including continuity of essential services such as ambulance, fire service, water supply, electricity, access for relief etc. through policy and procedures, knowledge transfer and capacity development, financing arrangement and partnership and will contribute to create environment for upscaling.

title.narrative: PIMS-10069 Umbrella Programme to Support NBSAP Update and the 7th National Reports NEPAL

description.narrative: This global programme will support countries in revising and updating their National Biodiversity Strategies and Action Plans (NBSAPs) in order to align with the recently agreed upon Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, and enable effective implementation of the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and the Kunming Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework at the national level. In addition, this grant supports countries in preparing and submitting their 7th National Report on the implementation of the Convention on Biological Diversity and the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework.

title.narrative: The Biodiversity Finance Initiative – Phase II (BIOFIN Nepal)

description.narrative: The Biodiversity Finance Initiative – BIOFIN – is a global programme that developed, piloted, and is continuously improving a methodology to measure existing biodiversity expenditure levels, assess future financial needs, and design comprehensive plans to use finance tools and solutions that increase financing, effectiveness and efficiency of biodiversity management in up to 132 countries. BIOFIN’s first phase (2012-2018) enabled full assessments and initial implementation of finance plan elements in target countries. Significant scaling up of finance solutions is required in all countries to address the biodiversity finance challenge.BIOFIN’s second phase enables up to 132 countries to complete the design and implementation of priority finance solutions and allowed a further 11 countries to undertake the assessments and create the finance plan. At the central level, knowledge management platforms will be expanded and additional guidance produced for most valuable finance solutions.The project aims to achieve the above-mentioned objectives through the following pathways and results:• Output 1: Effective advocacy and partnership strategy informs biodiversity finance practices• Output 2: Knowledge management infrastructure in place to generate and share knowledge on biodiversity finance that capacitate practitioners and decision makers• Output 3: Enhanced data and market intelligence database and index for biodiversity finance practitioners• Output 4: Prioritised biodiversity finance solutions create improved institutional and market frameworks to sustainably manage biodiversity at the national level• Output 5: BIOFIN process implemented at the national level in additional new countries• Output 6: Technical capacities for biodiversity finance assessment and planning process strengthened(non BIOFIN countries)

title.narrative: Provincial and Local Governance Strengthening Programme - Technical Assistance

description.narrative: TA will focus on providing technical assistance to the Provincial and Local Governance Strengthening Programme (PLSGP) for the institutional and organizational capacities of Provincial and Local Governments (PLGs) to deliver inclusive basic services (BSD) which benefit all people, especially women, the most vulnerable and marginalized, and for the federal government to develop and implement necessary laws and policies to strengthen inter-governmental relationships. The Government of Nepal and UNDP Nepal cost-shared portfolio will deploy a collaborative capacity development process and a Gender Equality and Social Inclusion (GESI) transformative approach to federal state building in all the seven provinces of Nepal. This systemic challenge requires a systems approach, which is best pursued through the framework of a portfolio.

title.narrative: Strengthening Health Resilience in Nepal - HIV, TB and Malaria

description.narrative: Nepal faces significant challenges in combating HIV, Tuberculosis (TB) and Malaria, despite noteworthy progress and achievement in public health and country’s response to health issues. According to the National Center for AIDS and STD control (NCASC), approximately 30,300 people live with HIV with concentrated prevalence among key populations such as sex workers, people who inject drugs, men who have sex with men and migrants who face higher risks due to unsafe practices and lack of targeted interventions. In 2023, 457 people were infected with HIV, a 91% reduction of annual infection rate compared to statistics in 2000. Whereas TB remains a major concern with an estimated incidence of 69,000, with only 69% of infected persons notified,1 and mortality among HIV negative persons of 17,000 Drug resistant TB (DRTB) is an emerging concern, further complicating prevention and treatment efforts. Nepal has started to implement a TB free initiative which has the potential to change the trajectory of the TB burden in the country. However, the initiative is currently limited to 150 out of 753 local governments. Limited or overburdened human resource capacity has markedly reduced the capacity to coordinate the TB programme implementation at the provincial level, and capacity development needs remain high. Further, the weak maintenance system of equipment including calibration, updating software versions and timely repair of equipment and diagnostics further challenge the disease treatment in Nepal. For the last decade Malaria cases have declined significantly but the disease remains endemic in 25 districts with over 1.2 million people who are at risk due to limited access to healthcare, particularly in remote areas of the country. Socio-economic factors, stigma, and poor health infrastructure all play major roles in timely treatment and access to the services. Further, low awareness, risk behaviours and funding gaps hinder Nepal’s ability to effectively prevent, diagnose, and

title.narrative: Strengthening Health Resilience in Nepal - HIV, TB and Malaria-UNDP Contribution

description.narrative: The Strengthening Health Resilience in Nepal - UNDP- HIV/TB and ML Project (the Project) will support the Government of Nepal (GoN) to strengthen the country’s resilience and health response and contribute towards a world free of the burden of HIV/AIDS, TB, and malaria with better and equitable health care for all. The project’s focus is to address the significant gaps in TB case notification by maximising efforts to reach missing cases with focus on screening among key and vulnerable populations and engaging private service providers. There is a need to improve the capacity of lab network especially in optimising sample transportation, diagnosis, utilisation of GeneXpert and ensuring quality assurance across the diagnostic lab network. Further, the Project will focus on expanding the treatment coverage for DRTB cases and bringing initial loss to follow up cases to radar of health care and treatment. Nepal’s shift to a federal system of governance, as outlined in the Constitution of 2015, has affected human resource capacities of TB and HIV/AIDS programmes, in particular at provincial and local levels. The Project aims to address this by supporting capacity development relevant stakeholders at the sub-national levels, thereby improving resilience of health systems to effectively response to HIV/TB and ML in the country.

description.narrative: The Green Transition in Nepal Portfolio (henceforth termed as Green Transition Portfolio) aims to support municipalities in Nepal in the adoption of green practices, focusing on three interconnected transformation pillars: 1. Developing circularity, and green and inclusive economy, adapted to each municipality’s context 2. Establishing a balanced relationship with nature by implementing sustainable and green municipal infrastructures and services, as well as improving the ecosystem resilience within and around municipalities to mitigate local climate and disaster risks3. Establishing supportive policy and governance environment to accelerate green transition in Nepal. It’s an umbrella portfolio that aims to engage with individual municipalities and build local level portfolios that are co-owned with the local authorities and communities, as well as promote coordination, learning, and sharing between them.

The project intervention is expected to improve major trekking trails and enhance various tourist services and facilities, promoting emerging destinations, building local capacities, and digitizing tourism services. These efforts are expected to foster inclusive, sustainable, and resilient economic development by creating tourism-based employment opportunities for women, youth, LGBTIQ people, and people living with disabilities including poor, vulnerable, and marginalized communities, capacity development of the tourism sector workforce, standardization of tourism services, adoption of digitization, and promotion of Nepals tourism sector.

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

title.narrative: mptf-Strengthening Inclusive Democratic Governance and Electoral Process in Nepal

description.narrative: This project supports Nepal’s preparation for the 2026 House of Representatives election by strengthening the Election Commission’s capacity to deliver credible, transparent, inclusive, and secure elections. It focuses on improving electoral readiness, enforcing the Code of Conduct, countering misinformation, enhancing voter education—especially for youth, women, and marginalized groups—and strengthening cybersecurity and data protection. The overall goal is to build public trust and ensure meaningful participation in the democratic process.

title.narrative: Inclusive energy access for accelerating economic growth in Karnali corridor (TA SASEC/ETDSP)

description.narrative: The TA will pilot the Build4Skills approach at selected project sites to facilitate job placement for local people. It will build on the prior experiences of UNDP, ADB, and Norwegian-funded projects to enhance the livelihoods of women and disadvantaged groups in Jumla, Dailekh, and Kalikot districts. It will also support institutionalizing gender equality and social inclusion (GESI) and implementing the gender action plan linked to the loan.

title.narrative: Smart buildings and green infrastructure for improved human security and sustainable development in Nepal

description.narrative: Nepal is warming at nearly twice the global average, with urban areas facing severe heat stress. In the southern Terai, extreme heat and humidity are driving rapid growth in energy demand for cooling, increasing emissions and placing significant stress on energy infrastructure. Dhangadhi Sub-Metropolitan City (DSMC) in Sudurpaschim Province experiences recurrent heatwaves during May–August, with temperatures frequently exceeding 40°C. Climate variability, rapid and unplanned urbanization, loss of green spaces, and limited energy-efficient infrastructure exacerbate risks to public health, livelihoods, and essential services, disproportionately affecting low-income households, outdoor workers, and residents of poorly insulated housing. Impacts include heat-related illness and mortality, school disruptions, and reduced functionality of public facilities. The objective of this Engagement Facility is to support the UNDP Nepal Country Office in operationalizing its digital positioning by translating it into a coherent, cross-portfolio digital transformation agenda and supporting its implementation across the Country Office. The expected output of the Facility is: UNDP Nepal’s digital positioning operationalized, strengthened and resourced through a coherent, cross-portfolio digital transformation agenda, enhanced implementation support. This will be achieved through strategic initiatives in three inter-linked areas: (1) Strategic Advisory and Coordination, (2) Partnerships and Ecosystem Development, and (3) Scalable Digital Interventions
